questions.text: There is no net transfer of energy by particles of medium in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a longitudinal wave, the particles of the medium oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Energy is transferred through compression and rarefaction of the particles. As the wave travels, the particles pass the energy to neighboring particles, resulting in a net transfer of energy.

A progressive wave refers to a wave that propagates through a medium, carrying energy from one point to another. It can be either a longitudinal or a transverse wave. In both cases, there is a net transfer of energy as the wave moves through the medium.

In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. Energy is transferred by the up-and-down motion of the particles. As the wave travels, the particles transmit the energy to neighboring particles, resulting in a net transfer of energy.

A stationary wave, also known as a standing wave, is formed by the superposition of two waves of the same frequency and amplitude traveling in opposite directions. In a stationary wave, the particles of the medium oscillate in fixed positions, resulting in regions of constructive and destructive interference. There is no net transfer of energy by the particles in a stationary wave.

questions.explanation: (a) Longitudinal wave: In a longitudinal wave, the particles of the medium oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. Energy is transferred through compression and rarefaction of the particles. As the wave travels, the particles pass the energy to neighboring particles, resulting in a net transfer of energy. (b) Progressive wave: A progressive wave refers to a wave that propagates through a medium, carrying energy from one point to another. It can be either a longitudinal or a transverse wave. In both cases, there is a net transfer of energy as the wave moves through the medium. (c) Transverse wave: In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. Energy is transferred by the up-and-down motion of the particles. As the wave travels, the particles transmit the energy to neighboring particles, resulting in a net transfer of energy. (d) Stationary wave: A stationary wave, also known as a standing wave, is formed by the superposition of two waves of the same frequency and amplitude traveling in opposite directions. In a stationary wave, the particles of the medium oscillate in fixed positions, resulting in regions of constructive and destructive interference. There is no net transfer of energy by the particles in a stationary wave. Out of these options, the correct answer is (d) stationary wave. Unlike longitudinal, progressive, and transverse waves, a stationary wave does not involve a net transfer of energy by the particles of the medium.

questions.text: The band theory of solids successfully classifies the solids into:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: These materials fall between conductors and insulators. Semiconductors have a smaller energy gap than insulators, allowing some electrons to move from the valence band to the conduction band with increased temperature or by other means (e.g., doping). This behavior enables semiconductors to exhibit moderate electrical conductivity, making them crucial in electronic devices and technology.

Materials categorized as conductors have partially filled or overlapping valence and conduction bands, allowing electrons to move easily between these bands. Metals are classic examples of conductors due to their high electrical conductivity.

Insulators have a large energy gap (forbidden band) between their valence and conduction bands. The energy required for electrons to jump from the valence band to the conduction band is typically high, preventing significant electron movement and resulting in very low electrical conductivity. Examples include materials like glass or rubber.

The band theory of solids successfully classifies solids into three main categories based on their electrical conductivity:Conductors, insulators and semiconductorsThe band theory of solids provides a framework for understanding the electrical behavior of materials based on their electronic band structures and helps classify solids into these three main categories based on their conductivity characteristics.

Metals are classic examples of conductors due to their high electrical conductivity.2. Insulators: Insulators have a large energy gap (forbidden band) between their valence and conduction bands. The energy required for electrons to jump from the valence band to the conduction band is typically high, preventing significant electron movement and resulting in very low electrical conductivity. Examples include materials like glass or rubber.3. Semiconductors: These materials fall between conductors and insulators. This behavior enables semiconductors to exhibit moderate electrical conductivity, making them crucial in electronic devices and technology.The band theory of solids provides a framework for understanding the electrical behavior of materials based on their electronic band structures and helps classify solids into these three main categories based on their conductivity characteristics.

questions.text: Which theory explains that energy exchange takes place in discrete “bundles” or “quanta”?

questions.explanation: The theory that explains that energy exchange takes place in discrete "bundles" or "quanta" is quantum theory. Subsequently, quantum theory was further developed by scientists like Albert Einstein, Niels Bohr, Werner Heisenberg, and Erwin Schrödinger, leading to the establishment of quantum mechanics. In quantum mechanics, the behavior of particles, including energy exchanges, is described in terms of discrete units or quanta.

questions.text: A body of mass 5 kg is moving with a momentum of 10 kg m/s. A force of 0.2 N acts on it in the direction of motion of the body for 10 s. The increase in it's kinetic energy is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is a numerical so it can only have one correct answer.
This is a numerical so it can only have one correct answer.
This is a numerical so it can only have one correct answer.
This is the correct option as per calculations.

questions.explanation: The initial momentum of the body is given as 10 kg m/s. Using the definition of momentum, we can find the initial velocity of the body as:p = mv10 = 5vv = 2 m/sThe force acting on the body is 0.2 N and it acts for a duration of 10 seconds. Therefore, the work done by the force is:W = Fd = (0.2 N)(2 m/s)(10 s) = 4 JThe change in kinetic energy of the body is equal to the work done by the force:ΔK = W = 4 JThe final kinetic energy of the body can be calculated using the formula:Kf = (1/2)mv^2Kf = (1/2)(5 kg)(4 m/s)^2 = 40 JThe increase in kinetic energy is therefore:ΔK = Kf - Ki = 40 J - (1/2)(5 kg)(2 m/s)^2 = 4.4 JTherefore, the increase in kinetic energy of the body is 4.4 J.

questions.text: Two point charges are sperated by a distance of 4m. The force between them is 4N. What is the force between the charges, When the distance between them is 1m?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect. According to Coulomb's Law, the force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance. Reducing the distance results in a significant increase in force.
Option B is correct. Using Coulomb's Law, the force increases by the square of the reduction in distance, resulting in a force of 64N.
Option C is incorrect. A reduction in distance leads to an increase in force, not a decrease.
Option D is incorrect. The force is not directly proportional to the distance, but rather inversely proportional to the square of the distance.

questions.explanation: Coulomb's Law states that the force between two point charges is directly proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Mathematically, this is expressed as F = K(Q1Q2)/r^2. Initially, the force is 4N when the distance is 4m. Therefore, K(Q1Q2)/(4^2) = 4. Solving for K(Q1Q2), we find K(Q1Q2) = 64. When the distance is reduced to 1m, the new force is calculated as K(Q1Q2)/(1^2) = 64, according to the earlier calculation. Thus, the correct answer is 64N. The other options are incorrect because they do not take into account the inverse square relationship dictated by Coulomb's Law.

questions.text: 12 J work is to be done against an existing electric field to take a charge of 0.01C from A to B. Find the potential difference between B and A:

The work done against an electric field is equal to the change in the electric potential energy of the charge. The change in the electric potential energy is also equal to the potential difference between the two points. In this case, the work done is 12 J, and the charge is 0.01 C. The potential difference between B and A is therefore: V_B - V_A = W / q = 12 J / 0.01 C = 1200 V Therefore, the potential difference between B and A is 1200 V.

questions.text: A boy standing in a lift falling freely under gravity releases a ball from his hand. As seen by him the ball:

options.explanation: Incorrect as the body appears stationary as both are accelerating down with the acceleration.

It is correct. When the lift falls freely under gravity (in free fall), everything inside the lift, including the boy and the ball, accelerates at the same rate due to gravity. Since both the boy and the ball are accelerating downward at the same rate, they effectively remain in the same relative position within the lift. Therefore, as observed by the boy, the ball would appear to remain stationary in the air when released from his hand. Gravity is acting equally on both the boy and the ball, causing them to fall together, and the ball does not exhibit any relative motion with respect to the boy in this scenario.

questions.explanation: Centripetal force is a force that acts radially inward on an object moving in a curved path, keeping it in that path. In the case of centripetal force, it always acts perpendicular to the displacement of the object, resulting in no work being done on the object.The work done by a force is calculated using the formula Work = Force x Distance x cos(θ), where θ is the angle between the force and displacement vectors. Since the angle is 90 degrees in the case of centripetal force, cos(90) = 0, leading to zero work done.Therefore, the correct answer is that centripetal force performs no work on the object, making option D the right choice. Options A, B, and C are incorrect as they do not consider the unique nature of centripetal force and its relationship to work.

questions.text: Statements: All water is liquid. Some liquid is acid. Conclusions: I. Some water is acid. II. Some acid is liquid.
